content:
Hi @Miguel Ojeda,

CC: @Philipp Stanner @Dave Airlie @Lyude @Andreas Hindborg @Maíra Canal

We got the first shot of topic branches for Rust abstractions in place.

They are located at three different remotes: nova, drm-misc and linux-rust-pci.

We have the following branches:

  • nova/nova
  • nova/rust-device
  • nova/rust-common
  • linux-rust-pci/rust-pci
  • drm-misc/topic/rust-drm

Both, rust-pci and topic/rust-drm, are based on rust-device, which simply contains a few generic device abstractions.

There is also nova/rust-common, which basically got rust-device, rust-pci and rust-drm merged and acts as the integration branch we talked about. nova/nova contains the nova driver stub, based on rust-common, making use of the PCI and DRM abstractions.

Corresponding to what we discussed, I think rust-device and rust-common are the candidates to move to the RfL repository. As for rust-pci, I don't really know if it will continue to live in RfL or if @Andreas Hindborg intends to keep it in his separate repository.

As already discussed the plan is to discuss and improve those abstractions and get them upstream via the Nova stub driver.

Given that, what is the preferred way to get some discussions about those abstractions going?

I think rust-device and rust-common are the candidates to move to the RfL repository. As for rust-pci, I don't really know if it will continue to live in RfL or if @Andreas Hindborg intends to keep it in his separate repository.

Up to you / @Andreas Hindborg! i.e. we are happy to create more branches if needed.

As already discussed the plan is to discuss and improve those abstractions and get them upstream via the Nova stub driver.

Sounds good.

Given that, what is the preferred way to get some discussions about those abstractions going?

If they are WIPs, then some ideas to get people involved are: reporting your progress e.g. here in Zulip and/or the relevant mailing lists from time to time, creating "good first issues" for newcomers, publishing articles and presenting talks and so on.

It would also be nice to have a subpage for Nova under "Users" in https://rust-for-linux.com (it would be yours and you can write your reports/status there, contact info, benchmarks, etc. -- please see the ones we already have there for inspiration: NVMe, Null block, Binder, PuzzleFS, AGX), and we can create extra streams here in Zulip if you find it useful etc.

When they are closer to upstreaming, then the best is to send the actual patches to the relevant mailing lists, of course.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I think rust-device and rust-common are the candidates to move to the RfL repository. As for rust-pci, I don't really know if it will continue to live in RfL or if @Andreas Hindborg intends to keep it in his separate repository.

Up to you / Andreas Hindborg! i.e. we are happy to create more branches if needed.

I don’t have any strong opinion for or against having rust-pci in the R4L GitHub org. One potential issue is that the branch is very much WIP :work_in_progress: and is not at all polished,. People might look at it and think that is what finished code should look like. A positive side effect of having it there is that it might get more exposure and perhaps people will decide to contribute to it.

We could keep it in the R4L org and rename it to rust-pci-wip, rust-pci-poc or something like that?

I know that there was a plan to get a minimal subset of nova merged upstream relatively soon. Is that near ready to send a RFC patchset? Some of the dependencies could probably start getting sent upstream shortly after

People might look at it and think that is what finished code should look like.

Indeed, that is a good point -- when we created the first ones we discussed having a sibling tree like linux-wip as an alternative too. But even if we don't do a different tree (or a branch name like you suggested), if a branch is "very WIP", then at least it should be clearly mentioned in its entry in the webpage and ideally in its README too.

Miguel Ojeda said:

I think rust-device and rust-common are the candidates to move to the RfL repository. As for rust-pci, I don't really know if it will continue to live in RfL or if @Andreas Hindborg intends to keep it in his separate repository.

Up to you / Andreas Hindborg! i.e. we are happy to create more branches if needed.

Great, can you push those two on RfL please?

Considering the comments below (finished vs. staging code) maybe we can just name them staging/rust-device and staging/rust-common? The term 'staging' should be rather unambiguous given that the kernel also has the staging subsystem.

As already discussed the plan is to discuss and improve those abstractions and get them upstream via the Nova stub driver.

Sounds good.

Given that, what is the preferred way to get some discussions about those abstractions going?

If they are WIPs, then some ideas to get people involved are: reporting your progress e.g. here in Zulip and/or the relevant mailing lists from time to time, creating "good first issues" for newcomers, publishing articles and presenting talks and so on.

Those are all great ideas. However, what I meant was more like how (and where) to get specific code discussed. Like with the branch naming above I didn't feel like pushing out patch series yet. However, I think discussing the code already seems to make sense.

I could think of sending it out tagged as staging ("[PATCH staging]") or simply send something similar to this original post to the mailing list asking for feedback by simply linking the branches, once created. Opinions?

It would also be nice to have a subpage for Nova under "Users" in https://rust-for-linux.com (it would be yours and you can write your reports/status there, contact info, benchmarks, etc. -- please see the ones we already have there for inspiration: NVMe, Null block, Binder, PuzzleFS, AGX), and we can create extra streams here in Zulip if you find it useful etc.

That sounds very good too. I think it would be a good start to document the current initial effort to get some basic (DRM) device and PCI abstraction upstream with the help of an initial Nova stub driver.

When they are closer to upstreaming, then the best is to send the actual patches to the relevant mailing lists, of course.

I'd also say that asking for feedback linking the branches on the standard mailing list is the best approach right now.
The list doesn't have that much traffic yet and the community has a good size, so that should work fine

I added the other day a mention of the fact that some of the branches may be WIP/staging in the "Topic branches" section: https://rust-for-linux.com/branches#topic-branches

I like the idea of a prefix or suffix like staging/ or -wip. I guess we may eventually need to rename them as non-staging ones when the code reach some maturity, but that is a good problem to have. Or, ideally, the subsystem maintainers agree at that point to add the branch into their own tree(s).

Between prefix/suffix, @Wedson Almeida Filho liked the prefix ("folder") approach since the branches would get sorted/grouped nicely. I also like it because, for the integration one, it allows us to avoid confusion with rust-dev (see below).

Ideally we would follow the same pattern for all branches, especially if they are all wip/staging at the moment. @Andreas Hindborg @Valentin Obst @Trevor Gross, what are your thoughts? How painful would it be if I rename your branches? I don't want to break any URLs you may have published already. But if you are all OK, I will move all topic branches to staging/ and create @Danilo Krummrich's too.

As for the integration one, what about staging/rust-dev since it mimics (in a way) rust-dev? By the time a staging/ topic branch matures and moves to non-staging, then the current rust-dev could be the place to integrate it anyway (up to @Boqun Feng and what he wants to accept/integrate within rust-dev, of course).

@Danilo Krummrich For the Nova page, I can upload the initial draft that you give me and then you can send easily updates. I will also link that page in the "Topic branches" section (like we did with Andreas'), since it is nice to mention the users of particular topic branches.
I think that having both staging/rust-dev and rust-dev will be confusing, why not use staging/rust?
Sounds good too.
Though we also have the bare rust, it may be confusing as well, but it is true that it served in the past a similar purpose too (though it didn't integrate other branches, we just did things there).

Benno Lossin said:

I think that having both staging/rust-dev and rust-dev will be confusing, why not use staging/rust?

Yea, I don't like the potential confusion with the old rust branch. How about staging/rust-dev-staging for a potential integration branch?

Miguel Ojeda said:

Ideally we would follow the same pattern for all branches, especially if they are all wip/staging at the moment. @Andreas Hindborg @Valentin Obst @Trevor Gross, what are your thoughts? How painful would it be if I rename your branches? I don't want to break any URLs you may have published already. But if you are all OK, I will move all topic branches to staging/ and create @Danilo Krummrich's too.

As

staging/ is fine by me :+1:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Benno Lossin said:

I think that having both staging/rust-dev and rust-dev will be confusing, why not use staging/rust?

Yea, I don't like the potential confusion with the old rust branch. How about staging/rust-dev-staging for a potential integration branch?

To me there is no real confusion with the old rust branch, since we already often add the prefix "old" when talking about it. I think staging/rust-dev-staging is too verbose.

Benno Lossin said:

Andreas Hindborg said:

Benno Lossin said:

I think that having both staging/rust-dev and rust-dev will be confusing, why not use staging/rust?

Yea, I don't like the potential confusion with the old rust branch. How about staging/rust-dev-staging for a potential integration branch?

To me there is no real confusion with the old rust branch, since we already often add the prefix "old" when talking about it. I think staging/rust-dev-staging is too verbose.

What does git checkout -b upstream/staging/rust-dev-staging do? Does it create a local staging/rust-dev-staging or just a rust-dev-staging? If it is the latter, naming the branch rust-dev without the suffix is no good.
AFAIK it would create staging/rust-dev-staging.

Benno Lossin said:

AFAIK it would create staging/rust-dev-staging.

Then I guess it's all fine with the less verbose one
staging/rust-dev is a merge point of all staging branches?

Boqun Feng said:

staging/rust-dev is a merge point of all staging branches?

That's the idea, yes.
so probably just staging-dev or staging/dev?

I always found "dev" confusing, as I always think of the kernel's struct device when I read it, which is why I proposed staging/rust-common originally, but I'm absolutely fine with any of the variants proposed. :)
Then staging/rust-merge?

For the future interaction with rust-dev, one thing I want to make clear is rust-dev will only contains patches that have been submitted to the mail list or in a subsystem tree which is watched by linux-next.
staging/int? staging/integration?
staging/merge or staging/rust-merge sounds OK too.
Also OK for me staging/dev.
All the above look good to me ;-)
@Danilo Krummrich Is dakr your GitHub nick? (for the invitation and setting up the access). Should I add @Philipp Stanner too?
@Miguel Ojeda wouldn't hurt. My name on github is "dasteihn"
Invitation sent!
Should I list both of you as maintainers of both branches in the webpage then?

Miguel Ojeda said:

Danilo Krummrich Is dakr your GitHub nick? (for the invitation and setting up the access). Should I add Philipp Stanner too?

Yes, it is.
Sent too!
@Danilo Krummrich You should now be able to push and force push to both those branches (and nothing else) -- if it doesn't work, please let me know!

Miguel Ojeda said:

Danilo Krummrich You should now be able to push and force push to both those branches (and nothing else) -- if it doesn't work, please let me know!

Thanks Miguel! Works as expected.
My pleasure!

Ideally we would follow the same pattern for all branches, especially if they are all wip/staging at the moment. @Andreas Hindborg @Valentin Obst @Trevor Gross, what are your thoughts?

Works for me :+1:

Miguel Ojeda said:

Should I list both of you as maintainers of both branches in the webpage then?

You could do that
